reason: The item cleanly tests boundary-aware interpretation of multi-observable nail penetration evidence (peak temperature vs flame duration, nail Joule heat magnitude, and cell-specific RISC thresholds) without requiring extra unstated variables. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, though some use absolutist language (“primarily,” “necessarily,” “always”) that can make elimination slightly stylistic rather than purely technical. Overall, it provides good discrimination for experts who can avoid mechanism inversion and scope overreach while integrating multiple reported metrics.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware reasoning about (i) causal initiators versus heat share, (ii) what convection can and cannot change (severity/timing vs onset temperature), and (iii) inference limits under strong internal temperature non-uniformity. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible, though some options use strong modal language (“directly,” “necessary and sufficient,” “limited impact”) that slightly increases guessability and reduces neutrality.

The prompt tightly constrains the required judgment to (i) relative impact magnitudes (350°C vs 50°C), (ii) a single mechanism explaining regime-dependent emissivity sensitivity (radiation vs convection dominance), and (iii) a careful non-overclaim on propagation prevention (“potential” vs guarantee). It strongly discriminates experts who can do boundary-aware heat-transfer reasoning and resist sufficiency overclaims, with only modest risk that “effective strategy” wording could invite subjective prioritization beyond what the evidence strictly supports.
